{{Short description|Phyletic symbiosis in microbiome research}}
{{Multiple issues|
{{more citations needed|date=July 2017}}
{{Technical|date=October 2021}}
}}
In the field of [[microbiome]] research, a group of [[species]] is said to show a '''phylosymbiotic''' signal if the degree of similarity between the species' microbiomes recapitulates to a significant extent their [[evolutionary history]].<ref>{{cite journal|last1=W. Brooks|first1=Andrew|title=Phylosymbiosis: Relationships and Functional Effects of Microbial Communities across Host Evolutionary History|journal=PLOS Biology|date=November 18, 2016|volume=14|issue=11|pages=e2000225|doi=10.1371/journal.pbio.2000225.g004|pmid=27861590|pmc=5115861|doi-access=free}}</ref>
In other words, a phylosymbiotic signal among a group of species is evident if their microbiome similarity [[dendrogram]] could prove to have significant similarities with their host's phylogenic tree. For the analysis of the phylosymbiotic signal to be reliable, environmental differences
that could shape the host microbiome should be either eliminated or accounted for.
One plausible mechanistic explanation for such phenomena could be, for example, a result of host immune genes that rapidly evolve in a continuous [[arms race]] with members of its microbiome.

== Animals ==
Across the animal kingdom there are many notable examples of phylosymbiosis. For instance, in non-human primates it was found that host evolutionary history had a substantially greater influence on the [[gut microbiome]] than either host dietary niche or geographic location.<ref>{{Cite Q|Q57735585}}</ref> It was speculated that changes in gut physiology within the evolutionary history of non-human primates was the primary reason. This finding was particularly interesting as it contradicted previous research which reported that dietary niche was a strong factor in determining the gut microbiome of mammals.<ref>{{Cite Q|Q35015637}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Gordon|first1=Jeffrey I.|last2=Knight|first2=Rob|last3=Schrenzel|first3=Mark D.|last4=Tucker|first4=Tammy A.|last5=Schlegel|first5=Michael L.|last6=Bircher|first6=J. Stephen|last7=Ramey|first7=Rob Roy|last8=Turnbaugh|first8=Peter J.|last9=Lozupone|first9=Catherine|date=2008-06-20|title=Evolution of Mammals and Their Gut Microbes|url= |journal=Science|language=en|volume=320|issue=5883|pages=1647–1651|doi=10.1126/science.1155725|issn=1095-9203|pmc=2649005|pmid=18497261|bibcode=2008Sci...320.1647L}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Gordon|first1=Jeffrey I.|last2=Knight|first2=Rob|last3=Henrissat|first3=Bernard|last4=Fontana|first4=Luigi|last5=González|first5=Antonio|last6=Clemente|first6=Jose C.|last7=Knights|first7=Dan|last8=Kuczynski|first8=Justin|last9=Muegge|first9=Brian D.|date=2011-05-20|title=Diet Drives Convergence in Gut Microbiome Functions Across Mammalian Phylogeny and Within Humans|url= |journal=Science|language=en|volume=332|issue=6032|pages=970–974|doi=10.1126/science.1198719|issn=1095-9203|pmc=3303602|pmid=21596990|bibcode=2011Sci...332..970M}}</ref>

== Plants ==
[[File:The impact of plant evolutions, domestication and breeding on the plant microbiome.jpg|thumb|457x457px|A conceptual figure on the impact of domestication on the plant endophytic microbiome. (a) A phylogenetic distance among ''Malus'' species which contains wild species (black branches) and progenitor wild species (blue branches). The extended green branch represents ''Malus domestica'' with its close affiliation its main ancestor (''M. sieversii''). Dashed lines indicate introgression events between ''Malus'' progenitors which contributed to the formation of ''M. domestica''. (b) The predicted three scenarios: Scenario 1, reduction in species diversity due to loss in microbial species; Scenario 2, increase in microbial diversity due to introgressive hybridization during the apple domestication; Scenario 3, diversity was not affected by domestication.]]
Plant kingdoms has shown strong relationships of phylosymbiosis with notable examples in  [[Malus]]<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=Abdelfattah |first1=Ahmed |last2=Tack |first2=Ayco J. M. |last3=Wasserman |first3=Birgit |last4=Liu |first4=Jia |last5=Berg |first5=Gabriele |last6=Norelli |first6=John |last7=Droby |first7=Samir |last8=Wisniewski |first8=Michael |year=2021 |title=Evidence for host–microbiome co-evolution in apple |journal=New Phytologist |language=en |volume=234 |issue=6 |pages=2088–2100 |doi=10.1111/nph.17820 |issn=1469-8137 |pmc=9299473 |pmid=34823272 |s2cid=244661193}}</ref> (apple family) and [[Poaceae]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Bouffaud |first1=Marie-Lara |last2=Poirier |first2=Marie-Andrée |last3=Muller |first3=Daniel |last4=Moënne-Loccoz |first4=Yvan |date=2014 |title=Root microbiome relates to plant host evolution in maize and other Poaceae |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/1462-2920.12442 |journal=Environmental Microbiology |language=en |volume=16 |issue=9 |pages=2804–2814 |bibcode=2014EnvMi..16.2804B |doi=10.1111/1462-2920.12442 |issn=1462-2920 |pmid=24588973|url-access=subscription }}</ref>  (grass family) species where endophytic communities mirror host evolutionary relationships. During plant domestication, three scenarios of phylosymbiotic patterns have been observed: reduction in microbial diversity, increased diversity through hybridization, or maintenance of existing diversity levels. In the Malus species case, including wild and domesticated cultivars, Malus harbored [[Endophyte|endophytic]] communities that corresponded to their [[Phylogenetics|phylogenetic]] relationship.<ref name=":0" />

== Factors that impact phylosymbiosis and distinction from coevolution ==
The concept of phylosymbiosis extends beyond simple correlation between host [[phylogeny]] and [[microbiome]] composition, encompassing deeper evolutionary implications.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Brooks |first1=Andrew W. |last2=Kohl |first2=Kevin D. |last3=Brucker |first3=Robert M. |last4=van Opstal |first4=Edward J. |last5=Bordenstein |first5=Seth R. |date=2016-11-18 |title=Phylosymbiosis: Relationships and Functional Effects of Microbial Communities across Host Evolutionary History |journal=PLOS Biology |volume=14 |issue=11 |article-number=e2000225 |doi=10.1371/journal.pbio.2000225 |doi-access=free |issn=1545-7885|pmc=5115861 }}</ref> One crucial aspect is the distinction between phylosymbiosis and [[coevolution]] - while phylosymbiosis describes a pattern of association between host evolutionary relationships and microbiome communities, it does not necessarily imply coevolution between hosts and their microbes.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Groussin |first1=Mathieu |last2=Mazel |first2=Florent |last3=Alm |first3=Eric J. |date=July 2020 |title=Co-evolution and Co-speciation of Host-Gut Bacteria Systems |journal=Cell Host & Microbe |volume=28 |issue=1 |pages=12–22 |doi=10.1016/j.chom.2020.06.013 |pmid=32645351 |issn=1931-3128|hdl=1721.1/133505.2 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> This distinction is particularly important because phylosymbiotic patterns can arise through various ecological and evolutionary processes, not all of which involve direct coevolutionary relationships.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Lim |first1=Shen Jean |last2=Bordenstein |first2=Seth R. |date=2020-03-04 |title=An introduction to phylosymbiosis |journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences |volume=287 |issue=1922 |article-number=20192900 |doi=10.1098/rspb.2019.2900 |pmid=32126958 |pmc=7126058 |issn=0962-8452}}</ref>

Vellend's four principles - selection, drift, [[speciation]], and dispersal  provide a comprehensive framework for understanding how phylosymbiotic patterns emerge in host-microbiome relationship<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Vellend |first=Mark |date=June 2010 |title=Conceptual synthesis in community ecology |journal=The Quarterly Review of Biology |volume=85 |issue=2 |pages=183–206 |doi=10.1086/652373 |issn=0033-5770 |pmid=20565040}}</ref> s. Selection impacts through host physiology and immune responses shapes microbial communities, while drift influences random population changes, speciation leads to host-specific adaptations, and dispersal affects microbiome transmission between hosts. These principles help explain why phylosymbiosis can exist without strict coevolution, as the observed patterns may result from any combination of these ecological processes rather than requiring direct evolutionary relationships between hosts and their microbiomes. Recent research has revealed that phylosymbiosis can be disrupted by various factors, including host diet, environmental changes, and disease states.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Foster |first1=Kevin R. |last2=Schluter |first2=Jonas |last3=Coyte |first3=Katharine Z. |last4=Rakoff-Nahoum |first4=Seth |date=2017-08-03 |title=The evolution of the host microbiome as an ecosystem on a leash |journal=Nature |volume=548 |issue=7665 |pages=43–51 |doi=10.1038/nature23292 |pmid=28770836 |pmc=5749636 |bibcode=2017Natur.548...43F |issn=0028-0836}}</ref> This disruption provides valuable insights into the stability and resilience of host-microbe associations. For instance, studies in Nasonia wasps have demonstrated that hybrid hosts often show broken phylosymbiotic patterns, suggesting that genetic incompatibilities between hosts and microbes can emerge during speciation.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Wang |first1=Jun |last2=Kalyan |first2=Shirin |last3=Steck |first3=Natalie |last4=Turner |first4=Leslie M. |last5=Harr |first5=Bettina |last6=Künzel |first6=Sven |last7=Vallier |first7=Marie |last8=Häsler |first8=Robert |last9=Franke |first9=Andre |last10=Oberg |first10=Hans-Heinrich |last11=Ibrahim |first11=Saleh M. |last12=Grassl |first12=Guntram A. |last13=Kabelitz |first13=Dieter |last14=Baines |first14=John F. |date=2015-03-04 |title=Analysis of intestinal microbiota in hybrid house mice reveals evolutionary divergence in a vertebrate hologenome |journal=Nature Communications |volume=6 |issue=1 |page=6440 |doi=10.1038/ncomms7440 |pmid=25737238 |pmc=4366507 |bibcode=2015NatCo...6.6440W |issn=2041-1723}}</ref> This observation has led to the hypothesis that phylosymbiosis might contribute to speciation through microbiome-mediated [[reproductive isolation]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Sharon |first1=Gil |last2=Segal |first2=Daniel |last3=Ringo |first3=John M. |last4=Hefetz |first4=Abraham |last5=Zilber-Rosenberg |first5=Ilana |last6=Rosenberg |first6=Eugene |date=November 2010 |title=Commensal bacteria play a role in mating preference of ''Drosophila melanogaster'' |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |volume=107 |issue=46 |pages=20051–20056 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1009906107 |doi-access=free |pmid=21041648 |pmc=2993361 |issn=0027-8424}}</ref>

The implications of phylosymbiosis for host health and adaptation are particularly relevant in the context of [[global change]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Alberdi |first1=Antton |last2=Aizpurua |first2=Ostaizka |last3=Bohmann |first3=Kristine |last4=Zepeda-Mendoza |first4=Marie Lisandra |last5=Gilbert |first5=M. Thomas P. |date=September 2016 |title=Do Vertebrate Gut Metagenomes Confer Rapid Ecological Adaptation? |journal=Trends in Ecology & Evolution |volume=31 |issue=9 |pages=689–699 |doi=10.1016/j.tree.2016.06.008 |pmid=27453351 |bibcode=2016TEcoE..31..689A |issn=0169-5347}}</ref> As species face novel environmental challenges, understanding how phylosymbiotic relationships influence host [[adaptation]] becomes increasingly important.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Moeller |first1=Andrew H. |last2=Suzuki |first2=Taichi A. |last3=Phifer-Rixey |first3=Megan |last4=Nachman |first4=Michael W. |date=2018-10-26 |title=Transmission modes of the mammalian gut microbiota |journal=Science |volume=362 |issue=6413 |pages=453–457 |doi=10.1126/science.aat7164 |pmid=30361372 |bibcode=2018Sci...362..453M |issn=0036-8075}}</ref> Research has shown that microbiomes can facilitate [[host adaptation]] to new environments, and the strength of phylosymbiotic relationships may influence this adaptive potential.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Morris |first=Jeffrey |date=2018-10-11 |title=Faculty Opinions recommendation of Hologenomic adaptations underlying the evolution of sanguivory in the common vampire bat. |doi=10.3410/f.732724927.793551602 |doi-access=free }}</ref> This has led to growing interest in using phylosymbiotic principles to inform conservation strategies and predict species' responses to environmental change.

== Investigational Methods ==
Methodologically, the study of phylosymbiosis has been revolutionized by advances in [[DNA sequencing|high-throughput sequencing]] technologies and bioinformatics tools.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Knight |first1=Rob |last2=Vrbanac |first2=Alison |last3=Taylor |first3=Bryn C. |last4=Aksenov |first4=Alexander |last5=Callewaert |first5=Chris |last6=Debelius |first6=Justine |last7=Gonzalez |first7=Antonio |last8=Kosciolek |first8=Tomasz |last9=McCall |first9=Laura-Isobel |last10=McDonald |first10=Daniel |last11=Melnik |first11=Alexey V. |last12=Morton |first12=James T. |last13=Navas |first13=Jose |last14=Quinn |first14=Robert A. |last15=Sanders |first15=Jon G. |date=2018-05-23 |title=Best practices for analysing microbiomes |journal=Nature Reviews Microbiology |volume=16 |issue=7 |pages=410–422 |doi=10.1038/s41579-018-0029-9 |pmid=29795328 |issn=1740-1526}}</ref> Modern analyses often employ sophisticated statistical approaches to account for the compositional nature of microbiome data and the complex hierarchical structure of host-microbe associations.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Gloor |first1=Gregory B. |last2=Macklaim |first2=Jean M. |last3=Pawlowsky-Glahn |first3=Vera |last4=Egozcue |first4=Juan J. |date=2017-11-15 |title=Microbiome Datasets Are Compositional: And This Is Not Optional |journal=Frontiers in Microbiology |volume=8 |page=2224 |doi=10.3389/fmicb.2017.02224 |doi-access=free |pmid=29187837 |pmc=5695134 |issn=1664-302X}}</ref> These methods include techniques such as distance-based redundancy analysis, structural equation modeling, and phylogenetic generalized linear mixed models, which help researchers disentangle the various factors contributing to phylosymbiotic patterns.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Morton |first1=James T. |last2=Marotz |first2=Clarisse |last3=Washburne |first3=Alex |last4=Silverman |first4=Justin |last5=Zaramela |first5=Livia S. |last6=Edlund |first6=Anna |last7=Zengler |first7=Karsten |last8=Knight |first8=Rob |date=2019-06-20 |title=Establishing microbial composition measurement standards with reference frames |journal=Nature Communications |volume=10 |issue=1 |page=2719 |doi=10.1038/s41467-019-10656-5 |pmid=31222023 |pmc=6586903 |bibcode=2019NatCo..10.2719M |issn=2041-1723}}</ref>

== Applications ==

=== Agricultural and Livestock ===
In agricultural contexts, phylosymbiosis has emerged as a valuable framework for crop improvement and pest management.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hacquard |first1=Stéphane |last2=Garrido-Oter |first2=Ruben |last3=González |first3=Antonio |last4=Spaepen |first4=Stijn |last5=Ackermann |first5=Gail |last6=Lebeis |first6=Sarah |last7=McHardy |first7=Alice C. |last8=Dangl |first8=Jeffrey L. |last9=Knight |first9=Rob |last10=Ley |first10=Ruth |last11=Schulze-Lefert |first11=Paul |date=May 2015 |title=Microbiota and Host Nutrition across Plant and Animal Kingdoms |journal=Cell Host & Microbe |volume=17 |issue=5 |pages=603–616 |doi=10.1016/j.chom.2015.04.009 |pmid=25974302 |issn=1931-3128|hdl=11858/00-001M-0000-0027-BFFA-A |hdl-access=free }}</ref> Understanding phylosymbiotic patterns in crop species can guide strategies for enhancing [[Plant defense against herbivory|plant resistance]] to pathogens and improving nutrient uptake efficiency.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Edwards |first1=Joseph |last2=Johnson |first2=Cameron |last3=Santos-Medellín |first3=Christian |last4=Lurie |first4=Eugene |last5=Podishetty |first5=Natraj Kumar |last6=Bhatnagar |first6=Srijak |last7=Eisen |first7=Jonathan A. |last8=Sundaresan |first8=Venkatesan |date=2015-01-20 |title=Structure, variation, and assembly of the root-associated microbiomes of rice |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |volume=112 |issue=8 |pages=E911-20 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1414592112 |doi-access=free |pmid=25605935 |pmc=4345613 |bibcode=2015PNAS..112E.911E |issn=0027-8424}}</ref> Similarly, in livestock management, phylosymbiotic insights are being used to optimize animal health through microbiome manipulation, taking into account the evolutionary relationships between host species and their associated microbial communities.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Wang |first1=Jun |last2=Nesengani |first2=Lucky T. |last3=Gong |first3=Yongsheng |last4=Yang |first4=Yujiang |last5=Lu |first5=Wenfa |date=2018-02-22 |title=16S rRNA gene sequencing reveals effects of photoperiod on cecal microbiota of broiler roosters |journal=PeerJ |volume=6 |article-number=e4390 |doi=10.7717/peerj.4390 |doi-access=free |pmid=29492337 |pmc=5825889 |issn=2167-8359}}</ref>

=== Personalized Medicine and Microbiome Therapeutics ===
Research into phylosymbiotic relationships has revealed that human genetic ancestry influences microbiome composition, which in turn affects [[drug metabolism]] and therapeutic outcomes.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=D |first1=Rothschild |last2=O |first2=Weissbrod |last3=E |first3=Barkan |last4=A |first4=Kurilshikov |last5=T |first5=Korem |last6=D |first6=Zeevi |last7=PI |first7=Costea |last8=A |first8=Godneva |last9=IN |first9=Kalka |last10=N |first10=Bar |date=2018-09-11 |title=Environment dominates over host genetics in shaping human gut microbiota |journal=Yearbook of Paediatric Endocrinology |doi=10.1530/ey.15.14.5 |issn=1662-4009}}</ref> This understanding has led to:

* Improved prediction of drug responses based on host-microbiome interactions<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Kundu |first1=Parag |last2=Blacher |first2=Eran |last3=Elinav |first3=Eran |last4=Pettersson |first4=Sven |date=December 2017 |title=Our Gut Microbiome: The Evolving Inner Self |journal=Cell |volume=171 |issue=7 |pages=1481–1493 |doi=10.1016/j.cell.2017.11.024 |pmid=29245010 |issn=0092-8674|hdl=10356/87346 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>

* [[Microbiome]]
